utterances: Jessie.
I mean it.

We're talking with Republicans about the endorsement yesterday of Barack Obama by General Colin Powell.
You're listening to Talk of the Nation from NPR News.
And here's an email we have from Justin in Concord, North Carolina.
I've been upset about the shrill tone of messages of the GOP, especially on immigration.
Upset enough to get involved on the balance of the issues, the GOP still has the best ideas.
But now is the time for people of principles to speak out and act against the run to the gutter.
And I don't think that Justin is alone there.
No, I think that's the main point.
We used to talk about when we created The Heritage Foundation, I was one of the three founders of The Heritage Foundation.

I was national chairman of the American Conservative Union, and while we were active there, we talked about the fact that we were the people with ideas and principles, and we believed in self government, and we were going to show how you can have a self government that was within the constraints of the Constitution, and divided power, and we could make that work.
And that's been lost, I mean, it is shrilled now, and it is all about trying to take down Obama and smear Obama.
And, you know, I am on the issues more of a Republican than a Democrat, I certainly agree far more with McCain on most of the issues that I do with Obama.
You know, but we've got to get out of the gutter that John has taken us down into.
Some people describe Barack Obama as a transformational candidate, because of the number of young people who've been so motivated to join his campaign.
Do you fear for a lost generation of Conservatives?
Well, maybe.
Although I would say it's transformational, not just with young people, because Susan Eisenhower is supporting Barack Obama.
Jim Leach, the former Republican congressman from Iowa, supporting Barack Obama.
So they're all - it's not just the young people, you know, it's just people like Colin Powell, it's people like myself.
I'm not - you know, I haven't endorsed anybody but, you know, it is a fact that a lot of people are moving over.
And you know what?
If the Republican Party can't stand for something better than this, then maybe people should leave it.
I mean, until it gets its act together and comes back.
I love the country more than I love my political party.
Now let's get Ed on the line, Ed's with us from Chester County in Pennsylvania.
Yeah.
Hi there.Yeah.
Hi.

I've been a Republican since ""I like Ike"" buttons, and since John's become a candidate starting with Sarah Palin, who I just feel is unqualified, I could've really written Colin Powell's message yesterday.
Every point he's hit, even negotiating with the enemy, is I believe the right path.
But as your speaker is saying today, the party is just going the wrong way.
The negative that - the personal attacks parts of the negative campaign.
Negative campaigning I think is part of, you have to attack the other person's programs, but not them personally.
And that's just way too far.
All right, Ed.
And have you voted in yet in Pennsylvania, or you're going to wait for the fourth?
No, we don't have early voting.
I'm registered, I have a ""Republican for Obama"" button on.
And I plan to volunteer for the Obama side to help get people out.
Ed, thanks very much for the call.
You know, Neal, if I can.
You know, one of the things that McCain brought up, and then Obama got into it too, was about Joe the Plumber.
Mm-hmm.
Well, you know, Joe, and Jill, and Cathy, and Charlie, and all the - you know, the people out there that - you know, who are concerned about where this country is going.

You know, they want to hear about, you know, what's going to be done about keeping the peace, or about Iraq, or about, you know, the economy, they don't want to hear about this guy was a bad guy, and you know, did you know his middle name is Hussein?
And you know, I think the American people, you know, are reacting as they should against that kind of a political campaign.
And where is this generation of idea's going to come from?
How is the party going to change?
How long is it going to take?
Well, I think one of the things that's going to happen - sometimes you have to have a defeat.
You have to get really wiped out, so you go back to the drawing boards.
Now here's the danger.
The danger is that if McCain loses by a substantial margin, and that could well happen, there's going to be a battle then for the soul of the party.
They are going to be people who feel like me, who feel that we have really blown it here.
And they are going to be people who are going to be like the Rush Limbaughs, who are going to say, see, you didn't listen to us.
You should've had somebody who is more hardcore right wing than John McCain.
So there'd be a battle over that.

And if we don't - if those of us who believe in government - but in limited government and self government - if we don't win that battle, I think the Republican Party is going to fade into irrelevancy.
It's going to be the Wig Party.
Mickey Edwards, thanks for your time today.
Thank you.

From NPR News, this is ALL THINGS CONSIDERED.

And I'm Robert Siegel.
Support for nationwide education standards known as the Common Core received another setback today.
Florida's governor announced that his state is stepping back from its role as one of the leaders of the effort.
Rick Scott, who faces re-election next year, says Florida will no longer serve as the fiscal agent for a group of states developing tests to measure student achievement.
As NPR's Greg Allen reports from Miami, Scott also says Florida will re-evaluate its commitment to the standards themselves.
Common Core began with a group of states and their governors, who wanted to improve the performance of American students.
Some 45 states agreed on the standards, and adopted them three years ago.
Now, states are working to develop a set of tests that will measure how well students are doing with the new standards.
Florida has been a key participant in one effort called the Partnership for Assessment of Readiness for College and Careers, or PARCC - that is, until today.
Florida Education Commissioner Pam Stewart made the announcement in a conference call with reporters.
We will be moving forward on withdrawing our responsibility as PARCC fiscal agent.
Under former governors Jeb Bush and then Charlie Crist, Florida has been a leader in the state movement to develop nationwide standards, and to test students on them.
But more recently in Florida and nationwide, conservative and Tea Party groups have been working to stop states from using Common Core and its tests.
Today, Gov.
Scott announced he's going not with his predecessors, but with the Tea Party.
Scott asked the head of the state's board of education to end Florida's role as fiscal agent for PARCC.
In a press release, he went further, saying he wants to pull out of the group altogether.
And he called for a series of public hearings on Common Core; hearings he said would examine, quote, ""risks for federal intrusion in Florida's standards.
Three was no federal involvement in developing the standards.
There was no federal money in developing the standards.
Chad Colby is with PARCC.
He says while no federal money helped create Common Core, the Department of Education did supply a $186 million grant to help develop the new tests.
With Florida's withdrawal as fiscal agent, a new state will be chosen to administer the grant.
And Colby expects the tests to be ready on schedule next spring.
Asked about what's changed since Common Core was first developed, Colby says it's the politics.
Anti-government fervor has proved to be a potent strain, especially among Tea Party groups that form an important part of Gov.
Scott's Republican base.
Speaking last week in Washington, Florida's former governor Jeb Bush called on elected leaders to rise above politics.
If you think that the standards of Florida, or any other state, are acceptable in a world that is incredibly competitive, that you want to benchmark yourself to mediocrity?
Or do you want to benchmark yourself to the best in the world?
If you're comfortable with mediocrity, fine.
I'm not.
Florida Education Commissioner Pam Stewart says Florida may end up choosing the PARCC tests, or go with a private contractor.
Either way, she says, she expects the state will have some tests ready to go by next spring.
Greg Allen, NPR News, Miami.
After learning of his firing, Joe Paterno made comments outside his home last night.
In his quiet voice, before a noisy crowd, he thanked students and well-wishers.
